CWE-119.name: Buffer Overflow
CWE-119.description: The program writes more data to a buffer than it can hold. This corrupts nearby memory and can allow attackers to execute malicious code.
CWE-119.why_dangerous: Attackers can overwrite memory to hijack program execution, escalate privileges, or crash the system. Used in many famous exploits like Morris Worm.
fix_steps: Always check input length before copying
Use safe functions: strncpy instead of strcpy

CWE-20.name: Improper Input Validation
CWE-20.description: The program does not properly validate input from users or external sources before using it. This allows attackers to inject malicious data.
CWE-20.why_dangerous: Unvalidated input can lead to SQL injection, command injection, buffer overflows, and many other attacks.
fix_steps: Check type, length, format, and range

CWE-89.name: SQL Injection
CWE-89.description: User input is directly inserted into SQL queries without sanitization. Attackers can manipulate the query to access or destroy the database.
CWE-89.why_dangerous: Attackers can read all database data, modify or delete records, bypass authentication, and sometimes execute commands on the server.
CWE-89.bad_code:
# VULNERABLE CODE
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + user_id
cursor.execute(query)
CWE-89.good_code:
# FIXED CODE
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s"
cursor.execute(query, (user_id,))  # Parameterized query
fix_steps: Never concatenate user input into SQL queries
Always use parameterized queries or prepared statements
Apply principle of least privilege to database accounts

CWE-79.name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
CWE-79.description: User input is displayed on a webpage without being sanitized. Attackers inject malicious JavaScript that runs in other users browsers.
CWE-79.why_dangerous: Attackers can steal session cookies, redirect users, deface websites, or perform actions on behalf of victims.
fix_steps: Always escape user input before displaying in HTML
Use template engines that auto-escape by default
Validate and sanitize all user input

CWE-78.name: OS Command Injection
CWE-78.description: User input is passed directly to system commands. Attackers can execute arbitrary commands on the server.
CWE-78.why_dangerous: Complete server takeover. Attackers can read files, install malware, create backdoors, or destroy the system.
CWE-78.bad_code:
# VULNERABLE CODE
import os
filename = request.args.get('file')
os.system(f"cat {filename}")  # DANGEROUS!
CWE-78.good_code:
# FIXED CODE
import subprocess
import shlex
filename = request.args.get('file')
# Validate filename first
if not filename.replace('_','').replace('-','').isalnum():
    return "Invalid filename"
result = subprocess.run(['cat', filename],
                       capture_output=True, text=True)
fix_steps: Never pass user input to shell commands
Use subprocess with list arguments not shell=True
Use language built-in functions instead of shell commands

CWE-22.name: Path Traversal
CWE-22.description: User input is used to construct file paths without validation. Attackers use ../ sequences to access files outside intended directory.
CWE-22.why_dangerous: Attackers can read sensitive files like /etc/passwd, configuration files with passwords, or private keys.
CWE-22.bad_code:
# VULNERABLE CODE
filename = request.args.get('file')
with open(f'/var/www/files/{filename}') as f:
    return f.read()
CWE-22.good_code:
# FIXED CODE
import os
filename = request.args.get('file')
base_dir = '/var/www/files/'
full_path = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(base_dir, filename))
# Check path is still inside base directory
if not full_path.startswith(base_dir):
    return "Access denied"
with open(full_path) as f:
    return f.read()
fix_steps: Use os.path.realpath to resolve the full path
Check that resolved path starts with allowed base directory

CWE-416.name: Use After Free
CWE-416.description: Memory is accessed after it has been freed. This can corrupt data or allow attackers to execute malicious code.
CWE-416.why_dangerous: Attackers can manipulate freed memory to execute arbitrary code or cause crashes.
fix_steps: Always set pointer to NULL after freeing
Use static analysis tools to detect use-after-free

CWE-125.name: Out-of-bounds Read
CWE-125.description: The program reads data past the end of an allocated buffer. Can expose sensitive memory contents.
CWE-125.why_dangerous: Can leak sensitive data like passwords or encryption keys from memory. Famous example: Heartbleed bug.
fix_steps: Always use strict less-than in array loops
Use sizeof to get actual buffer size

CWE-190.name: Integer Overflow
CWE-190.description: An integer calculation produces a result too large for the variable type. The value wraps around to a small or negative number.
CWE-190.why_dangerous: Can bypass security checks, cause buffer overflows, or lead to incorrect program behavior.
fix_steps: Check for overflow before arithmetic operations
Use larger integer types when needed

CWE-476.name: NULL Pointer Dereference
CWE-476.description: The program dereferences a pointer that is NULL. This causes a crash or unexpected behavior.
CWE-476.why_dangerous: Can crash the program causing denial of service. In some cases can be exploited for code execution.
fix_steps: Always check pointer for NULL before dereferencing
Check return values of functions that can return NULL

CWE-362.name: Race Condition
CWE-362.description: Two threads access shared data simultaneously without proper synchronization. Results are unpredictable.
CWE-362.why_dangerous: Can cause data corruption, privilege escalation, or security bypass depending on the context.
CWE-362.bad_code:
# VULNERABLE CODE
balance = get_balance()
if balance >= amount:
    withdraw(amount)  # Another thread can modify between check and withdraw!
CWE-362.good_code:
# FIXED CODE
import threading
lock = threading.Lock()

with lock:  # Atomic check-and-withdraw
    balance = get_balance()
    if balance >= amount:
        withdraw(amount)

CWE-200.name: Information Exposure
CWE-200.description: The program accidentally exposes sensitive information to unauthorized users through error messages, logs, or responses.
CWE-200.why_dangerous: Leaks passwords, API keys, system paths, or internal logic that helps attackers plan their attack.
fix_steps: Never expose stack traces to users
Use generic error messages for users

CWE-264.name: Permissions and Privileges
CWE-264.description: The program does not properly manage permissions or privileges, allowing unauthorized access to resources.
CWE-264.why_dangerous: Users can access data they should not see, escalate privileges, or perform unauthorized actions.
CWE-264.bad_code:
# VULNERABLE CODE
def delete_user(user_id):
    db.execute(f"DELETE FROM users WHERE id={user_id}")
    # No permission check!
fix_steps: Always check permissions before sensitive operations

CWE-399.name: Resource Management Error
CWE-399.description: The program does not properly manage resources like memory, file handles, or network connections.
CWE-399.why_dangerous: Resource leaks cause system slowdown, crashes, or denial of service over time.
CWE-399.bad_code:
# VULNERABLE CODE
def read_file(path):
    f = open(path)
    data = f.read()
    return data  # File handle never closed!
CWE-399.good_code:
# FIXED CODE
def read_file(path):
    with open(path) as f:  # Auto-closes
        data = f.read()
    return data
fix_steps: Always close file handles, connections, and resources
Use context managers (with statements in Python)

CWE-798.name: Hardcoded Credentials
CWE-798.description: Passwords, API keys, or other secrets are hardcoded directly in the source code.
CWE-798.why_dangerous: Anyone with access to the code can see the credentials. Common cause of data breaches when code is pushed to public GitHub.
CWE-798.good_code:
# FIXED CODE
import os
DB_PASSWORD = os.environ.get("DB_PASSWORD")
API_KEY = os.environ.get("API_KEY")
# Store secrets in .env file (never commit to git!)
fix_steps: Never hardcode credentials in source code
Use secrets management tools like AWS Secrets Manager
